weaknesses: Devotion: Bound by loyalty to Vishnu
Serpent Curse: Ancient enmity with nagas makes those encounters volatile
Dharmic Restriction: Cannot act against cosmic order
name: Hindu Mythology
abilities: Divine Flight: Unlimited speed and mobility in all realms
Strength: Capable of carrying mountains and gods
Serpent Dominion: Can perceive and combat serpent creatures
Vitality Transfer: Can grant or restore vital energy
Multi-realm Travel: Can traverse physical and spiritual worlds
description: Semi-divine serpent beings inhabiting Patala, guardians of treasures and sacred waters. Major nagas include Shesha, Vasuki, Takshaka, and Kaliya.
habitat: Heavens, sacred mountains, temples dedicated to Vishnu
behavior: Noble and righteous, devoted to Vishnu, protector of dharma (cosmic order)

christian.overview: Hindu Nagas as serpent deities with complex moral characters invite comparison with biblical serpent imagery spanning from Genesis to Revelation.

biblicalParallels.description: The serpent as tempter contrasts with benevolent Nagas; both traditions recognize serpents as wise beings.
The bronze serpent lifted to heal prefigures Christ. This redemptive imagery parallels healing Nagas.
Christ's counsel to be "wise as serpents" recognizes positive serpent qualities like Naga wisdom.

patristicInterpretation.summary: Church Fathers distinguished between the deceiving serpent and Moses' healing serpent.

sources.author: Justin Martyr
sources.interpretation: The bronze serpent prefigures Christ taking the curse of sin.

comparativeTheology.nagarjuna: The Buddhist philosopher Nagarjuna ("Arjuna of the Nagas") allegedly received teachings from Nagas - paralleling how Scripture often associates serpents with hidden wisdom.
comparativeTheology.waterSymbolism: Nagas as water guardians parallel baptismal symbolism of the deep.

christian.typologicalReading: Naga guardianship of treasures finds Christian parallel in Christ as keeper of wisdom's treasures (Colossians 2:3).
scholarlyReferences: Vogel, J. Ph. Indian Serpent-Lore. London: Arthur Probsthain, 1926.
Charlesworth, James H. The Good and Evil Serpent. Yale University Press, 2010.

keyMyths.title: Samudra Manthana (Churning of the Ocean)
keyMyths.description: Vasuki, the king of the Nagas, was used as the rope by the gods (Devas) and demons (Asuras) to churn the ocean of milk, leading to the emergence of Amrita (the nectar of immortality) and other treasures. This event highlights the Naga's immense strength and willingness to participate in cosmic events.

keyMyths.title: Shesha Naga and Vishnu's Rest
keyMyths.description: Shesha Naga, the king of all Nagas, serves as the couch upon which Lord Vishnu rests in the cosmic ocean between cycles of creation and destruction. Shesha's coils represent the endless nature of time and the cyclical nature of the universe.

keyMyths.title: Takshaka and Parikshit's Death
keyMyths.description: Takshaka, a powerful Naga, was responsible for the death of King Parikshit, the grandson of Arjuna, as retribution for the king disrespecting a sage. This story illustrates the Nagas' capacity for vengeance and their power to inflict death.

keyMyths.title: Kaliya's Subjugation by Krishna
keyMyths.description: The young Krishna subdued the venomous Naga Kaliya, who had poisoned the Yamuna River. Krishna danced on Kaliya's heads until the Naga begged for forgiveness, demonstrating Krishna's divine power over even the most formidable serpents.

keyMyths.title: Arjuna and Ulupi
keyMyths.description: Arjuna, during his exile, married Ulupi, a Naga princess. Their union resulted in the birth of Iravan, a valiant warrior who played a significant role in the Mahabharata war, showcasing the Nagas' ability to intermarry with humans and produce powerful offspring.

sources.name: Mahabharata
sources.description: One of the two major Sanskrit epics of ancient India, containing numerous stories and references to Nagas.
sources.title: Mahabharata

sources.name: Vishnu Purana
sources.description: One of the eighteen major Puranas, focusing on the stories and attributes of Lord Vishnu and including details about Nagas, particularly Shesha.
sources.title: Vishnu Purana

sources.name: Bhagavata Purana
sources.description: Also known as Srimad Bhagavatam, this Purana narrates the stories of Krishna and other avatars of Vishnu, including the tale of Kaliya.
sources.title: Bhagavata Purana

sources.name: The Serpent and the Rope: A Study of Indian Symbolism
sources.description: A scholarly work by Rene Guenon exploring the symbolism of the serpent in various cultures, including Hindu traditions, and the Naga's role.
sources.title: The Serpent and the Rope: A Study of Indian Symbolism

sources.name: Hindu Myths: A Sourcebook
sources.description: Translated by Wendy Doniger O'Flaherty, this book compiles and translates key Hindu myths, including those featuring Nagas, providing insights into their significance.
sources.title: Hindu Myths: A Sourcebook
